topic: Ghanaian jollof rice debate — his recipe vs Abena's

turns.content: I need an impartial opinion on a matter of considerable domestic importance.
That sounds serious. What's the matter?
Jollof rice. Specifically, whose jollof rice is superior — mine or my wife's. We have been having this debate for the better part of two decades and I fear we are no closer to resolution.

A two-decade jollof rice dispute is not something to take lightly. I suspect you both believe you're right with equal conviction. What's the core disagreement — is it about ingredients, technique, or the final result?

It is all three, which is what makes it so intractable. Abena is a purist. She uses a very traditional approach — the tomato base is cooked down until it is nearly black, which gives it that smoky depth. She insists on basmati rice, which she rinses until the water runs perfectly clear. The result is precise. Every grain separate. The flavor concentrated. It is, I will admit privately and never to her face, technically excellent.
So you're conceding technical excellence while clearly positioning yourself to argue for something else. What does your version bring to the table that hers doesn't?

Mine is more generous. I use more tomato paste, more onion, and I add a touch of nutmeg which is not traditional but which I believe elevates the dish. I also use jasmine rice rather than basmati. It is slightly stickier, which means the grains absorb the sauce more fully. Her jollof is a composition. Mine is an embrace. Both are valid approaches to rice.

"Her jollof is a composition, mine is an embrace" — that's a genuinely poetic way to describe two approaches to rice. The nutmeg is an interesting addition. It's not traditional, but nutmeg has a way of rounding out tomato-based sauces without announcing itself. Does she object to the nutmeg specifically, or to the principle of improvisation?

Ah but that is the heart of it. She objects to the principle. Abena believes that the recipe as passed down by her mother, and her mother's mother, is not to be altered. It is a heritage, not a suggestion. When I add nutmeg, she looks at me the way a pharmacist looks at someone who has modified their own prescription. With deep professional concern.

That comparison is perfect. And honestly, she has a point about heritage recipes carrying a kind of authority. But so do you — cooking traditions evolve, and the best cooks have always been the ones who respect the foundation but aren't afraid to add something of themselves. Has anyone outside the household ever been asked to weigh in, or is this strictly a bilateral negotiation?

We hosted some friends from church last month and I made the mistake of putting both versions on the table simultaneously. I thought it would be a definitive test. Instead, it split the room exactly in half and created two additional arguments among the guests. One gentleman declared that both were inferior to his mother's version in Takoradi, which nearly caused an international incident in our dining room.

Introducing a third competitor into a jollof dispute is a bold move by that guest. That's the thing about jollof — everyone's mother's version is the definitive one, and suggesting otherwise is practically a diplomatic provocation. It sounds like the taste test didn't resolve anything but at least confirmed that this is a universal debate, not just a household one.

It confirmed that jollof rice is the one subject on which reasonable people cannot find consensus. I have resolved complex surgical decisions with less disagreement. But I will say this — the evening was wonderful. The house smelled extraordinary, the conversation was lively, and at the end of the night every grain of both versions was gone. Perhaps that is the only verdict that matters.

Empty pots are the most honest review. If both versions disappeared completely, you're both winning — just in different ways. And it sounds like the real point of the evening wasn't settling the debate but enjoying the table together. Though I suspect neither of you will concede that out loud.
You are correct. The debate will continue. It is one of the enduring pleasures of our marriage. Thank you for listening to what may be the least consequential problem I have ever brought to you.

Not every conversation has to be consequential. Sometimes the ones about jollof rice and kitchen diplomacy are the best ones. Enjoy the ongoing debate — it sounds like neither of you would have it any other way.
